food_subgroup: Bone-in mutton (goat) pieces in spiced onion-tomato curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Indian Mutton Curry. Nearest parent is cooked goat meat in spiced sauce. A new concept for Indian bone-in goat curry is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Goat meat curry, bone-in, South Asian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A08HM used as closest parent for bone-in goat/mutton curry in South Asian preparation style; no exact code exists for Indian mutton curry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Indian mutton curry as a prepared dish.

allergens: dairy (yogurt — may be present in some preparations)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one bowl), which includes bone-in mutton pieces and curry gravy, as commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Curry — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F06060011; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Food Code F06060011 from HPB Singapore Foodo database is an exact match for Indian Mutton Curry. Values cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for goat meat curry entries. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Meat-Based Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry entries; GI estimated at 20 consistent with low-carbohydrate meat-dominant dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian Mutton Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate based on the low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100g) and the dominance of protein and fat, which are known to suppress glycaemic response. This is consistent with GI values reported for similar low-carbohydrate meat-based curry dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (20 × 11.25) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 963 mg per standard 250 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit mutton curry to a small portion of 100–150 g per meal, request reduced salt during cooking, and note that the potassium content (285 mg/100g) provides partial mitigation but does not offset the high sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100g) and low glycemic index (estimated GI 20)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Indian Mutton Curry is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and low GI; the high protein (14.5 g/100g) and fat content promote satiety and blunt postprandial glycaemic response, though sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (4.2 g/100g) and cholesterol (72 mg/100g) combined with high sodium (385 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Indian Mutton Curry in moderation (100–150 g portions), as the saturated fat and sodium content may adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; the high zinc (4.5 mg/100g) and selenium (22 mcg/100g) content offer antioxidant benefit but do not offset the cardiovascular risk factors at large serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in red meat may promote pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; offset by high zinc, B12, and selenium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton curry may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the high zinc (4.5 mg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg/100g), and selenium (22 mcg/100g) support immune function, but the arachidonic acid content of red meat warrants limiting frequency to 2–3 times per week.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Curry (Indian) is a robust, hearty, and deeply aromatic curry dish made from bone-in goat meat (commonly called mutton in South Asia) slow-cooked or pressure-cooked in a rich, dark-reddish gravy. The gravy base — known as 'bhuna masala' — is built by sautéing onions until golden, then adding ginger-garlic paste, tomatoes, and a full South Asian spice blend comprising turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder, cumin, and garam masala. Yogurt is often added for creaminess and to tenderise the meat. The bone-in preparation is traditional and imparts a deep, marrow-rich flavour that distinguishes it from boneless preparations. The dish is pan-Indian with regional variations: Bengali preparations (Mangshor Jhol) are lighter and brothier; Tamil preparations (Mutton Kuzhambu) are darker and more pungent with curry leaves and black pepper; North Indian versions are richer with cream or nut pastes. Nutritionally, Indian Mutton Curry is high in protein (14.5 g/100g), moderate in fat (10.8 g/100g), and very low in carbohydrates (4.5 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. It is exceptionally rich in zinc (4.5 mg/100g), vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg/100g), and selenium (22 mcg/100g), nutrients critical for immune function and neurological health. Sodium is high at 385 mg/100g (963 mg per 250 g bowl), making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. Available at Indian restaurants, South Indian restaurants, and Indian Muslim (Mamak) eateries across Singapore. Traditionally served with steamed rice, roti, or paratha.
common_user_queries: mutton curry vs chicken curry nutrition
is mutton curry good for weight loss

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database under exact food code F06060011, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2. GI is an analogous estimate (not a direct measurement) which reduces GI sub-component confidence to medium, but overall record confidence remains high due to strong macro and micronutrient provenance.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Indian mutton curry is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (Food Code F06060011) or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables at the level of individual fatty acid classes for this prepared dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared curry dish is not reported in the HPB or ICMR-NIN sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Food Code F06060011). GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Indian Mutton Curry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (20 × 11.25) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3, based on 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 250g serving. Portions deduplicated: input contained four portion entries, three of which were identical duplicates; only two distinct portions are retained (1 bowl 250g verified; 1 serving 250g enriched_json). Copper (0.12 mg/100g), manganese (0.08 mg/100g), and vitamin_k (1.2 mcg/100g) sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9; these values are plausible for a meat-based curry and are retained. Vitamin_a reported as 22.0 mcg RAE per 100g, consistent with spice and tomato contribution in the curry base.
